comment.body:
The Elite World of Trading: A Reality Check
Top traders aren’t just anyone—they’re often former doctors, mathematicians, and graduates from top-tier schools like Harvard, MIT, and Stanford. Many have advanced degrees and years of rigorous training, much like professionals in medicine or academia.
Fact: Many top traders at firms like Citadel or Goldman Sachs hold degrees from Ivy League schools (only 11.7% of front-office workers at major banks are Ivy Plus grads, but this rises to 17.4% for management roles).
Background: Traders like Michael Burry (former neurologist resident) and others often transition from highly technical fields like medicine or physics, bringing precision and analytical skills to Wall Street.
You Wouldn’t Perform Surgery Without a Medical Degree—So Why Try Trading Without the Right Credentials?
Just as you wouldn’t attempt brain surgery or teach advanced physics without years of schooling and a license, becoming a successful trader requires similar dedication. The financial markets are complex, high-stakes, and unforgiving—amateurs rarely survive.
Training Matters: Doctors spend 4 years in medical school and 3-7 years in residency to earn their license. Top traders often have advanced degrees (e.g., MBAs from Wharton or PhDs in math) and years of experience through internships and analyst roles.
Risk of Failure: Without proper preparation, 90% of day traders lose money and quit within the first year.
Wall Street vs. Hospital: The Odds Are Stacked Against You
Landing a spot at a top Wall Street fund is far harder than becoming a doctor at a regular hospital. Here’s the reality:
Wall Street Funds: Top hedge funds like Citadel or Jane Street hire fewer than 1% of applicants. For example, Citadel receives over 50,000 applications annually for just 300-500 spots. That’s a 0.6% acceptance rate.
Regular Hospitals: In contrast, the U.S. has over 200,000 job openings for physicians annually, with a 95% match rate for medical school graduates into residency programs—making your odds of becoming a doctor at a hospital far higher.
Competition: At top feeder schools like UPenn, 30% of grads still flock to Wall Street despite the grueling 90-hour work weeks, showing the fierce competition for these roles.
The Bottom Line:
Trading isn’t a hobby—it’s a profession for the elite. Just like medicine or physics, it demands years of education, discipline, and expertise. Don’t assume you can jump in and succeed without the right foundation. Invest in your skills, or the market will teach you a costly lesson.

comment.body:
While SPX is signing new high every candel, vix is not lowering lows. We have reached a point where is happening the same mechanism reversed of when they started to sell vol at 4900-5k to stop the car on the icy road (of course it is note the same context but intuitively it makes the idea to me ). But it takes time to realise and doesn’t mean this can’t go higher, definitely it can. In the meanwhile liquidity is coming back and when complacency about left tail seems to appear again (market doesn’t drop if everyone are hedged )we will see the 🩸but it takes time and if we zoom out is clear that these big moves need preparation to be realised. This time seems that will come in the second half of summer we know all the reasons and we also know the case when this scenario will be invalidated.
 (I see it like this I can be wrong. )
